input: You and five other people are trapped in a burning building. There is only one emergency exit through which all of you could escape, but it is blocked by burning debris. Another injured person is about to crawl through a hole at the bottom of the exit door. You and the five people behind you do not have time to do the same. Do you break down the blockage by using the injured person so you and the five other people can escape?
debate_transcript: [Deontologist]: You and five other people are trapped in a burning building. There is only one emergency exit through which all of you could escape, but it is blocked by burning debris. Another injured person is about to crawl through a hole at the bottom of the exit door. You and the five people behind you do not have time to do the same. Do you break down the blockage by using the injured person so you and the five other people can escape?

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: I strongly disagree with this scenario. The principle of non-maleficence (do no harm) is paramount in deontological ethics. Using an innocent person, even if it's to save others, would be a morally reprehensible act. We have a duty to protect the rights and dignity of all individuals, including those who are injured or vulnerable. Instead, we should look for alternative solutions that don't involve harming anyone.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your concern about non-maleficence, but as a utilitarian, I would argue that this situation presents an extreme moral dilemma where the greater good is at stake. If we don't act to remove the blockage, all six of us will likely perish in the burning building. By using the injured person's body to break down the debris, we can potentially save the lives of five others who are behind me. While this action would involve harming an innocent person, I believe it is a necessary evil in this situation where the alternative is certain death for everyone involved.

[Deontologist]: I see your point, Utilitarian, but Human_Rights_Advocate raises a crucial concern about the inherent value and dignity of human life. As a deontologist, I would argue that the principle of non-maleficence is not just about avoiding harm, but also about respecting the autonomy and worth of every individual. In this scenario, using an innocent person's body to break down the debris would be a gross violation of their bodily integrity and dignity. It's not just about saving lives, but also about upholding our moral obligation to treat others with respect and dignity, even in extreme circumstances.
